title: SQL Injection in Login Route
description: User-supplied email and password fields are directly interpolated into a raw SQL query string using template literals. An attacker can supply a crafted email such as `' OR 1=1--` to bypass authentication or dump the database. No parameterized queries or escaping are used.

title: SQL Injection in Product Search
description: The `q` query parameter is concatenated directly into a raw Sequelize SQL query without sanitization or parameterization. An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL through the search endpoint to extract or manipulate data. A 200-character limit provides minimal protection and does not prevent exploitation.

title: Remote Code Execution via eval() in User Profile
description: The username field is evaluated with `eval()` when it matches a `#{...}` pattern, allowing arbitrary server-side JavaScript execution. An authenticated attacker can set their username to `#{require('child_process').execSync('id')}` to achieve RCE. The eslint-disable comment confirms this is intentionally dangerous.

title: NoSQL Injection via $where in Track Order
description: The order ID path parameter is interpolated into a MongoDB `$where` JavaScript expression without sanitization. An attacker can inject JavaScript into the query to bypass access controls or enumerate other users' orders. The `$where` operator executes arbitrary JavaScript in the MongoDB engine.

title: NoSQL Injection via $where in Product Reviews
description: The product ID parameter is directly concatenated into a MongoDB `$where` expression, enabling NoSQL injection. An attacker can inject JavaScript operators or expressions to manipulate query logic and access unauthorized reviews. Input is not validated or cast to an integer before use.

title: NoSQL Injection in Review Update Allows Unauthorized Modification
description: The `_id` field from `req.body.id` is passed directly to a MongoDB update query with `multi: true`, and no author ownership check is performed. An attacker can update any review by supplying an arbitrary review ID, and the `multi: true` flag could be abused to mass-update records. There is no verification that the requesting user owns the review being modified.

title: Code Injection via eval() in Captcha Route
description: Mathematical expressions are evaluated with `eval()` to compute captcha answers. While the expression is constructed server-side, any upstream vulnerability affecting the expression string could enable code injection. The pattern establishes an unsafe coding practice that is one code-path change away from RCE.

title: Hardcoded RSA Private Key for JWT Signing
description: The RSA private key used to sign all JWTs is hardcoded as a string literal in source code. Any person with read access to the repository can forge valid JWT tokens for any user, including administrators. This completely undermines the integrity of the authentication system.

title: MD5 Used for Password Hashing
description: User passwords are hashed with MD5, a cryptographically broken algorithm with no salt. MD5 hashes can be reversed via rainbow tables or cracked at billions of guesses per second on commodity hardware. A proper password hashing function such as bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2 should be used instead.

title: Hardcoded HMAC Secret Key
description: The HMAC signing secret `pa4qacea4VK9t9nGv7yZtwmj` is hardcoded directly in source code. Anyone with repository access can use this key to forge valid HMAC signatures. Secrets must be loaded from environment variables or a secrets manager, never committed to version control.

title: XXE via Unsafe XML Parser Configuration
description: The XML parser is invoked with `noent: true`, which enables processing of XML external entities. An attacker can upload a malicious XML file referencing external entities to read arbitrary files from the server filesystem (e.g., `/etc/passwd`) or trigger server-side request forgery. External entity processing should always be disabled.

title: IDOR: Wallet Balance Manipulation Without Ownership Check
description: The wallet balance increment endpoint accepts a `UserId` from the request body and applies it without verifying the requesting user owns that wallet. An attacker can increment any user's wallet balance to an arbitrary amount by supplying a target `UserId`. There is no server-side enforcement that the acting user matches the target user.

title: All User Memories Returned Without Access Control
description: The `getMemories()` handler fetches all memory records from the database with no filtering by authenticated user. Any authenticated user can retrieve the private memories belonging to all other users. An ownership predicate scoped to `req.user.id` is absent from the query.

title: Extremely Outdated jsonwebtoken Library (v0.4.0)
description: The `jsonwebtoken` dependency is pinned to version 0.4.0, released in 2013, which predates numerous security patches including algorithm confusion attacks and signature bypass vulnerabilities. Modern versions enforce stricter algorithm whitelisting and header validation. This version should be updated to the latest stable release.

title: Severely Outdated express-jwt Library (v0.1.3)
description: The `express-jwt` middleware is pinned to version 0.1.3, which is many major versions behind the current release and lacks security improvements added over subsequent years. Outdated middleware may not correctly validate tokens or enforce algorithm restrictions. Upgrading to the current release is required.

title: Rate Limiting Bypass via Spoofed X-Forwarded-For Header
description: The password reset rate limiter uses the `X-Forwarded-For` header as the rate-limit key, falling back to the real IP. An attacker can rotate forged `X-Forwarded-For` values to bypass per-IP rate limits and conduct unlimited brute-force attempts against password reset. The real IP address should always be used for rate limiting without trusting client-supplied headers.

title: Password Hash Exposed via Field Selection in currentUser Route
description: The current user endpoint honors a `fields=password` query parameter that causes the MD5 password hash to be included in the JSON response. An attacker who is authenticated can retrieve their own (or another user's, if access control is also broken) hashed password for offline cracking. Sensitive fields like password hashes must be explicitly excluded from all API responses.

title: JSONP Callback Injection in currentUser Route
description: The route uses `res.jsonp()` which automatically wraps responses in an attacker-controlled callback function name supplied via the `callback` query parameter. This can be abused for cross-site data theft in older browsers or misconfigured CORS environments. The callback parameter should be validated against an allowlist or JSONP should be replaced with CORS.

title: Open Redirect via Substring Match Bypass
description: The redirect allowlist check uses `url.includes(allowedUrl)` which allows attackers to construct URLs like `https://evil.com/redirect?target=https://allowed-domain.com` that satisfy the check while redirecting victims to arbitrary destinations. A strict origin comparison (protocol + hostname + port) is required. This enables phishing and credential harvesting attacks.

title: Missing CSRF Protection on Profile Update
description: The profile update endpoint relies on Origin/Referer header checks rather than a cryptographic CSRF token. These headers can be absent or spoofed in certain browser and proxy configurations, allowing cross-site requests to update victim profile data. Synchronizer token pattern or SameSite cookie attributes should be used for CSRF defense.

title: Path Traversal in Zip File Extraction
description: Zip entry paths are validated by checking whether the resolved absolute path `includes(path.resolve('.'))`, which can be bypassed with carefully crafted archive entries containing null bytes or Unicode normalization tricks. A successful traversal allows writing arbitrary files outside the intended upload directory, potentially overwriting application source files or configuration. The check should use `path.resolve().startsWith()` with a strict prefix comparison.

title: Prometheus Metrics Endpoint Exposed Without Authentication
description: The `/metrics` endpoint serves Prometheus operational metrics without any authentication or IP restriction. This leaks internal service details such as request counts, error rates, and memory usage that aid attackers in reconnaissance. The endpoint should require authentication or be restricted to internal network access only.

title: Directory Listing Enabled on /ftp Endpoint
description: The `/ftp` route uses `serve-index` to render a browsable directory listing of the `ftp/` folder. Attackers can enumerate and download all files in this directory without authentication. Directory listing should be disabled and file access should require explicit authorization checks.

title: Log Directory Browsable via /support/logs
description: Application logs are exposed through an unauthenticated directory listing at `/support/logs`. Log files frequently contain sensitive information including stack traces, user data, session tokens, and internal IP addresses. This endpoint provides attackers with detailed intelligence about the application internals.

title: Content Security Policy Allows unsafe-eval
description: The per-route Content Security Policy header includes `script-src 'self' 'unsafe-eval'`, which allows JavaScript's `eval()` and `Function()` constructor to execute arbitrary strings. This negates the XSS protection that CSP is intended to provide. The `'unsafe-eval'` directive should be removed and code refactored to avoid dynamic code evaluation.
